summary: My father came to the United States in 1903 when he was 18 years old, arriving first in Seattle before moving down to California to work as a farm laborer. The United States became our home, where my mother joined us after coming as a picture bride. During World War II, despite the hardships and Executive Order 9066, we remained connected to this country, and I even served in the Military Intelligence Service to help during the occupation of Japan.

Seattle was the first place my father landed when he came to the United States in 1903. From there, he traveled down to California to find work as a farm laborer. Seattle was the gateway for many Japanese immigrants like my father who sought new opportunities in America.

California was where my family finally settled after my father came to the United States. He worked up and down the Valley as a farm laborer before settling in Livingston between 1910 and 1913. California was also the center of our farming activities, especially in sweet potatoes and grapes, and it was where the Yamato Colony was established for Japanese immigrants.

Livingston is where my father settled around 1910 to 1913, becoming part of the Yamato Colony, a Japanese farming community. This area was chosen because of its good water and farming potential, especially for sweet potatoes and grapes. During the war, Livingston's cooperative system helped protect our land while we were interned, which was a great relief to my family.

Japan was my father's homeland, specifically Wakayama Prefecture, where he originally intended to join the military but was rejected due to a hearing issue. My mother was a teacher in Japan before coming to the United States as a picture bride. Later, during World War II, I served in the occupation of Japan, witnessing firsthand the devastation in places like Tokyo and Yokohama.

My father came from Wakayama Prefecture in Japan, where he and his cousin went to Tokyo to take a military officer test. Although he passed the written exam, he was rejected because of a deaf ear, which ultimately led him to leave Japan for the United States. Wakayama was the place he left behind when he started his new life in America.

Yamato Colony was the Japanese farming community where my family settled in the early 1900s near Livingston, California. It was started by Mr. Abiko from San Francisco around 1906, who bought land to create a place for Japanese immigrants to farm and live. The name came from a local 'yam station' near Atwater combined with the name Atwater itself, and the community was unique for being Christian rather than Buddhist.

San Francisco was home to Mr. Abiko, who was instrumental in founding the Yamato Colony by purchasing land in California for Japanese immigrants. He was also involved in the newspaper business, starting the Nichi Bei. The city was a hub for Japanese Americans before many moved to farming communities like Livingston.

Nichibei was the newspaper started by Mr. Abiko in San Francisco, who was a well-educated man and a leader in the Japanese American community. The paper was part of his efforts to support Japanese immigrants, including founding the Yamato Colony. Nichibei played a role in connecting and informing the community during those early years.

Cressy is a small community adjacent to Livingston and was part of the early Japanese farming settlements starting around 1906, just after the San Francisco earthquake. It was one of the two original communities alongside Livingston in the Yamato Colony area. During the war years, Cressy was included in the cooperative trusteeship that protected Japanese American land while families were interned.

Atwater is near the Yamato Colony and was part of the inspiration for its name, which combined 'yam station' near Atwater with the name itself. The area around Atwater, including Livingston and Cressy, was known for farming, especially sweet potatoes. It was part of the agricultural region where my family lived and worked.

California was the state where my family made their life after immigrating from Japan. We farmed in the Central Valley, particularly in the Yamato Colony around Livingston, Cressy, and Cortez. California was important to us not only for its agriculture but also because of the cooperative system that helped protect our land during World War II internment.

The 442nd Infantry Regiment was the first group recruited from the camps during World War II, and many of my friends joined it. It was a segregated Japanese American unit known for its bravery. Although I did not serve in the 442nd, I was aware of its importance and the pride it brought to our community.

I volunteered for the Military Intelligence Service in November 1943 and was sent to Camp Savage in Minnesota to learn Japanese. The MIS was a critical unit during World War II, training Nisei to serve as translators and interpreters. I was part of this group that later participated in the occupation of Japan, translating military documents and assisting Allied forces.

The Presidio of San Francisco was where the Military Intelligence Service language school was originally started in October 1941. John Aiso was the head of the language program there before it moved to Camp Savage. The Presidio played an important role in preparing Japanese Americans like me for service during the war.

John Aiso was the head of the Military Intelligence Service language school and became a Lieutenant Colonel. He was a well-educated man who had studied in both Japan and the United States. After the war, he went on to become a judge. His leadership was crucial in training Nisei soldiers like me in Japanese language skills.

Camp Savage in Minnesota was where I was sent in November 1943 to attend the Military Intelligence Service language school. The camp became the new location for the MIS school after it moved from the Presidio. It was a tough school, especially learning Kanji, but it prepared us for our roles in the war and occupation of Japan.

Executive Order 9066 was signed during World War II and led to the internment of Japanese Americans, including my family. It imposed curfews and restricted movement, but in our community around Livingston, Cressy, and Cortez, the cooperative trusteeship helped protect our land during this difficult time. The order changed our lives drastically, but we tried to hold on to what we had.

Many of my friends in the Military Intelligence Service were from Hawaii, and they had an easier time learning Japanese because they grew up speaking it. Unlike us mainland Nisei, they had more exposure to the language, which helped them in the intensive language school. Hawaii was an important source of personnel for the MIS during the war.

The Allied Translator and Interpreter Section, or ATIS, was the unit I was assigned to after completing language school. We were stationed near Manila in the Philippines in July 1945, just before the atomic bombings. Later, we were sent to Japan as part of the occupation forces to translate military documents and assist with interrogations.

Manila was where I was stationed with the Allied Translator and Interpreter Section in July 1945, shortly before the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki. We were there to prepare for the occupation of Japan, and it was a key staging area for our deployment. Manila was an important location in the final months of the war.

entity: Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki
summary: The atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki in August 1945 were a turning point in the war. I was stationed near Manila at the time, and shortly after the bombings, we were sent into Japan as part of the occupation forces. The devastation we saw in Tokyo and other cities was immense, and it shaped much of our work during the occupation.

Yokohama was the port where we arrived when entering Japan as part of the occupation forces in September 1945. From there, we took a train to Tokyo, passing through miles of blackened, burnt-out areas caused by the war. It was a stark and unforgettable introduction to post-war Japan.

Tokyo was where I spent the first winter of the occupation after arriving in Japan in 1945. The city was heavily damaged by incendiary bombings, leaving much of it burnt and blackened. I remember seeing many homeless children wandering the streets and subways, which was a very sad sight. Despite this, we were treated well by the Japanese people because we looked like them but wore American uniforms.

We were stationed in the Nippon Yusen Kaisha (NYK) building in Tokyo during the occupation. NYK was a major Japanese shipping company, and its building served as our base of operations. Being there gave us a close-up view of the devastated city and the challenges Japan faced after the war.

General Douglas MacArthur was the Supreme Commander of the Allied Powers during the occupation of Japan. I was stationed in the General Headquarters building, the Daiichi building, where he worked. I interpreted for a British major there and was involved in some interrogations related to Japanese military and secret societies.

The Black Dragon Society was a secretive Japanese organization before the war, and during my time in Japan, I helped interpret interrogations involving its members. I accompanied a British major to interview the head of the society. It was a tense experience, especially when tea was offered and the major was afraid of being poisoned, but I had no problem drinking it.
